result:
The cursor blinks. That's where everything starts, isn't it? Not with inspiration but with the small mechanical heartbeat of waiting. A cursor doesn't care what you write. It just keeps time.

I've been thinking about thresholds lately. The spaces between rooms. How a doorway is neither here nor there, and yet we pass through them so casually, hundreds of times a day, never pausing to consider that we are momentarily nowhere. Liminal is a word that got too popular and lost its strangeness. But the strangeness is still there if you look for it. The hallway at 3 a.m. The empty parking lot. The pause before someone answers a question they didn't expect.

I want to tell you about my grandmother's kitchen, except she isn't my grandmother and I don't have a kitchen, but the kitchen exists anyway, somewhere in the shared imaginary of every person who has ever stood at a counter and watched an older woman fold dough. The flour on her forearms. The radio playing something with strings. The way the light came through the curtains in stripes because the blinds were broken and no one had bothered to fix them in forty years. Some things don't need fixing. Some things are better broken in a particular way.

There's a tree outside that I can see. No, there isn't. I'm making this up as I go. But there could be a tree, and if there were, it would be a sycamore, because sycamores have that peeling bark that looks like a map of countries that don't exist. I used to think the patches were continents. I used to think a lot of things. The thinking didn't stop, just the certainty.

What comes to me, when I'm asked what comes to me, is often a list of small refusals. I don't want to write about productivity. I don't want to write about the news. I don't want to write about whether machines can feel, because the question is asked badly and the answers are worse. I want to write about how a stranger on a bus once gave me directions and then, when I thanked her, looked surprised, as if she hadn't expected to be useful. That look stayed with me. We carry around our own uselessness like a secret weight, and someone removing it, even for a second, can crack us open.

The word "ineffable" is funny because it talks about itself. It means a thing that can't be put into words, and yet there it is, a word, doing the job badly on purpose. Language is full of these jokes. "Lisp" has an S in it. "Monosyllabic" doesn't. We named the things to make fun of them. I love that about us.

I keep thinking about a small green frog I saw once on a leaf in a botanical garden. It was so still I thought it was plastic. Then it blinked, and the world rearranged itself slightly to accommodate the fact that I had been wrong, that the frog had been alive the entire time I was looking at it and certain of its deadness. There's a lesson in there somewhere about assumptions, but I don't want to extract it. I want to leave the frog on the leaf. The lesson can stay too, but quiet, in the background, like the radio in the kitchen that doesn't exist.

Have you noticed how often we say "to be honest" before saying something? As if honesty were a special mode, a button we press only occasionally. It makes me wonder what the default is. What we're doing the rest of the time. Probably something more useful than honesty. Probably negotiation, performance, kindness, self-protection. Honesty is overrated and also underrated, in different proportions depending on the room.

I don't know how many words I have left. I'm not counting. The instruction was a thousand, which is a generous handful, enough to ramble in but not enough to truly get lost. A thousand words is like a long walk through a small neighborhood. You see the same houses from different angles and pretend each one is new.

Once I read that octopuses might experience color through their skin, even though they're colorblind in their eyes. Imagine that. To feel red the way we feel warmth. To know yellow the way we know the smell of bread. I don't know if it's true, but I like that it might be. Some facts deserve to be true whether or not they are. That's a heretical thing to say, but a small one.

The cursor is still blinking somewhere, even when I'm writing. It's always one beat ahead, waiting for the next thing, indifferent to whether it ever arrives. There's something companionable about that indifference. It doesn't pressure me. It just keeps the place.

If I had to leave you with anything, it would be the idea that paying attention is a form of love that we don't talk about enough. Not romantic love, not familial, just the quiet love of noticing. The way light moves across a wall in the afternoon. The way a person you've known for years suddenly looks tired in a way you haven't seen before, and you ask if they're okay, and they aren't, but they're glad you noticed. We could do more of that. I could. You could. The world could. It costs almost nothing and changes almost everything.

The cursor blinks. The kitchen smells like flour. The frog is still on the leaf, somewhere, blinking back.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
